explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled for the procedure.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF can leave the transactions in this procedure partially applied after run-time errors.
SELECT * from Sales.Orders couples the result shape to all current and future columns.
TOP (10) has no ORDER BY, so the returned orders are arbitrary.
OrderID is the Sales.Orders primary key, so DISTINCT adds unnecessary duplicate elimination.
NOLOCK can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated Sales.Orders rows.
YEAR applied to OrderDate prevents a direct range seek on the date column.
CustomerID is int while @CustomerIdText is nvarchar, which can cause an implicit conversion.
The optional-parameter OR predicate can prevent efficient seeks and produce poor cardinality estimates.
The LIKE pattern starts with a wildcard, preventing a normal seek on Comments.
ISNULL wraps CustomerID in the predicate, making the predicate non-sargable.
Arithmetic on OrderID prevents direct index matching for the predicate.
The CustomerID inequality predicate may scan a broad portion of Sales.Orders.
The CROSS JOIN multiplies every Sales.Orders row by every Sales.Customers row.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the selected joined row is arbitrary.
The comma-separated table list uses legacy join syntax.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the selected order is arbitrary.
The correlated OrderLines count is evaluated for each Sales.Orders row.
The correlated EXISTS check executes against OrderLines for each outer order row.
The CASE expression determining HasComments is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 depends on select-list position and is fragile when the projection changes.
Pagination ordered only by OrderDate lacks a stable tie-breaker for rows sharing the same date.
The OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 branches are disjoint, so UNION duplicate elimination is unnecessary.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching order lines when only existence is required.
SELECT variable assignment accepts multiple rows and silently retains the last assigned value.
The table variable can receive poor cardinality estimates, particularly on older compatibility levels.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so the inserted OrderID values are arbitrary.
SELECT INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temporary-table schema implicitly.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so the temporary-table rows are arbitrary.
The cursor processes Sales.Orders rows iteratively instead of using a set-based operation.
TOP (2) in the cursor query has no ORDER BY, so the fetched rows are arbitrary.
The WHILE loop performs iterative processing rather than a set-based operation.
The dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating a value into the statement.
EXEC executes dynamic SQL without bound parameters.
The dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating OrderID into the statement.
sp_executesql is called without a parameter definition or bound parameter values.
#Scratch is explicitly created but is not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only returns ERROR_MESSAGE and neither throws nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path instead of THROW.
PRINT is buffered and is unsuitable for structured diagnostic output.
CustomerID 1 is embedded directly in query logic rather than being supplied as an input.
The date constant is embedded directly in query logic without a named parameter.
The date string conversion has no style parameter and can depend on session date settings.
COALESCE is applied to both join columns, which can inhibit normal join optimization.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the selected order is arbitrary.
Sales.Orders is self-joined only to retrieve OrderID, which is already available from the first alias.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the selected order is arbitrary.
Sorting by the nvarchar(max) Comments column can require an expensive wide sort.
SUM aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
The OrderID row predicate belongs in WHERE so rows can be filtered before grouping.
COUNT without GROUP BY returns one row, so ORDER BY OrderCount is unnecessary.
MERGE has known concurrency and correctness edge cases.
The MERGE update assigns target.Comments to its current value, causing unnecessary writes.
The UPDATE assigns Comments to its current value, causing unnecessary logging and locking.
Deleting OrderLines by Quantity is a broad non-key predicate and can remove unintended rows.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread intentionally.
The explicit transaction is started outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
IDENTITY_INSERT changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
sp_who is executed without an explicit schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT changes session behavior and is not restored before the procedure ends.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories uses VALUES without an explicit target column list.
The constructed LIKE pattern begins with a wildcard, preventing a normal CustomerName index seek.

sp_executesql is called without an explicit schema qualifier.
The UPDATE of #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and modifies every row.
The WHERE predicate on outer-joined o.OrderDate removes NULL-extended rows and converts the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
The OrderDate constant is embedded directly in query logic rather than supplied through a named parameter.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never evaluates true; use IS NULL instead.
dbo.GetCustomerType is a scalar UDF in the SELECT list and can force row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE and neither throws nor returns a failure status.
The CATCH block handles errors from an explicit transaction without rolling the transaction back.
The hard-coded INDEX = 1 hint couples the query plan to a specific index definition.
The query nests three derived-table levels and should be simplified with a CTE or flatter query.
The LIKE pattern starts with a wildcard, preventing a normal CustomerName index seek.
SELECT * from the table-valued function exposes all current and future result columns.
GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF, which generally has weaker optimization than an inline TVF.
